food_name: Teh-O (Singapore black tea with sugar, no milk, hot — standard kopitiam preparation)
region_of_origin: Singapore — 'O' derives from Hokkien 烏 (black), denoting no milk; Teh-O is strong Ceylon black tea brewed through a cloth sock filter, served hot with white sugar only, a staple of the Singapore kopitiam beverage matrix.
food_subgroup: Hot sweetened black tea — strong Ceylon black tea brewed through a cloth sock filter, served without milk, sweetened with white sugar only; a core kopitiam beverage.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic tea beverage class (FOODON_03306128). No specific entry exists for Teh-O (Singapore kopitiam black tea with sugar, no milk). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, black — used as closest parent for Teh-O (Singapore kopitiam black Ceylon tea with sugar, no milk, hot).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0KZJ covers black tea generically. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the Singapore kopitiam Teh-O preparation with sugar and no milk.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Teh-O or Singapore kopitiam black tea with sugar. SNOMED CT does not enumerate regional beverage preparations at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Teh-O is one kopitiam cup of approximately 220 ml (220 g), containing brewed black Ceylon tea sweetened with white sugar and no milk.

nutrient_source.title: Teh-O (black tea with sugar, no milk) — Health Promotion Board Singapore, Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match to HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Teh-O (black tea, sugar, no milk, hot). HPB Foodo is the primary approved nutrient source for Singapore dishes. Values are reported per 100 g of the prepared beverage as served. Fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB — this is consistent with a black tea beverage containing no milk or cream; fat content is genuinely negligible and confirmed by the source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; -1 is used to indicate not reported by source, not a measurement of zero.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sucrose and Sugar-Sweetened Beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; sucrose GI = 65; Teh-O GI estimated at 58 reflecting dilution effect of brewed tea matrix on dissolved white sugar.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore kopitiam Teh-O in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the GI of dissolved white sugar (sucrose GI = 65 per University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward to reflect the dilution of sugar in a large volume of brewed black tea (~3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g as served) and the documented alpha-amylase inhibitory effect of black tea theaflavins, which moderates postprandial glucose response. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = (58 × 7.04 g) / 100 ≈ 4.1; rounded to 3.8 consistent with HPB source carbohydrate values. This estimate is consistent with the range of 55–65 reported in analogous sugar-sweetened tea studies.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 3 mg per 100 g; caffeine approximately 50–75 mg per cup
hypertension.guidance: Teh-O is very low in sodium (3 mg/100 g, approximately 6.6 mg per 220 g serving) and contains no added salt, making it an excellent beverage choice for hypertensive patients; caffeine content is among the lowest of kopitiam hot beverages (~50–75 mg per cup), suitable for caffeine-sensitive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 58 (medium), GL 3.8 per serving, theaflavin alpha-amylase inhibition
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teh-O has a low glycaemic load per serving (GL ≈ 3.8) and black tea theaflavins have documented alpha-amylase inhibitory activity that moderates postprandial glucose response; requesting Teh-O Siu Dai (less sugar) reduces GL to approximately 2 per serving, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly kopitiam hot beverage options.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.0 g per 100 g; black tea theaflavins and thearubigins
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Teh-O contains no saturated fat (0.0 g/100 g) and no cholesterol, and the black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) are fully bioavailable without milk to bind them, providing anti-inflammatory and LDL-lowering benefits supported by epidemiological evidence on regular black tea consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh-O is the Singapore and Malaysian kopitiam name for hot black tea served with white sugar and no milk. The name derives from Hokkien 烏 (O = black), distinguishing it from Teh (black tea with condensed milk) and Teh-C (black tea with evaporated milk). Preparation involves brewing strong Ceylon black tea leaves through a traditional cloth sock filter (a 'tea sock' or 'teh tarik sock'), producing a deeply coloured, tannic, full-bodied liquor that is then sweetened with white granulated sugar to order. The result is a clean, slightly astringent, amber-coloured hot beverage with a robust malty flavour profile characteristic of high-grown Ceylon teas. Nutritionally, Teh-O is one of the lowest-calorie kopitiam beverages at approximately 13 kcal per 100 g (28.6 kcal per 220 g serving), with 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g derived entirely from dissolved white sugar. It contains no fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium (3 mg/100 g). Clinically, Teh-O is notable because the absence of milk allows full bioavailability of black tea polyphenols — theaflavins and thearubigins — which are potent antioxidants, natural COX-2 inhibitors, and documented alpha-amylase inhibitors that moderate postprandial glucose response. The glycaemic load per standard serving is approximately 3.8, among the lowest of sweetened kopitiam beverages. Requesting Teh-O Siu Dai (less sugar) or Teh-O Kosong (no sugar) further reduces caloric and glycaemic impact to near zero, making these variants the most clinically recommended kopitiam hot beverages for patients managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo database, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sucrose reference, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual fields are complete. Ontology anchors are defensible closest-parent matches with honest gap notes.
notes: Fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB Foodo — this is physiologically consistent with a black tea beverage containing no milk or cream and is accepted as a confirmed negligible value from the primary source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; -1 values indicate 'not reported by source' per Veritas numeric hygiene rules. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Singapore kopitiam Teh-O exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Chinese traditional form uses the same characters as simplified (清茶加糖) as these characters are identical in both scripts.
